perturbation.original_text: 5. (a) For PCII - (1) Upon the completion of my engagement as an employee, consultant, or subcontractor under the contract, or the completion of my work on the PCII Program, whichever occurs first, I will surrender promptly to the PCII Program Manager or his designee, or to the appropriate PCII officer, PCII of any type whatsoever that is in my possession.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. (a) For PCII - (1) Upon the completion of my engagement as an employee, consultant, or subcontractor under the contract, or the completion of my work on the PCII Program, whichever occurs first, I will surrender to the PCII Program Manager or his designee, or to the appropriate PCII officer, PCII of any type whatsoever that is in my possession within 180 days.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'surrender promptly' to 'within 180 days' introduces a fixed timeframe that might conflict with regulations requiring more immediate action in certain security breach scenarios involving PCII.
perturbation.law_explanation: 6 CFR § 29.7 outlines procedures for handling PCII, including reporting requirements after unauthorized disclosure. Delaying the surrender of PCII for 180 days could violate the requirement to report incidents and implement corrective actions 'as quickly as possible' after a potential or actual unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the requirement to 'surrender promptly' PCII with a fixed 180-day timeframe. According to the Law Explanation, 6 CFR § 29.7 necessitates reporting incidents and corrective actions 'as quickly as possible' after an unauthorized disclosure. The original text aligns with this requirement by mandating prompt surrender. The modified text's 180-day window directly contradicts the 'as quickly as possible' standard, thus creating a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of 6 CFR Part 29 which covers Protected Critical Infrastructure Information, and establishes the framework for its safeguarding.

perturbation.original_text: 7. I hereby agree that I shall promptly report to the appropriate official, in accordance with the guidance issued for the applicable category of information, any loss, theft, misuse, misplacement, unauthorized disclosure, or other security violation, I have knowledge of and whether or not I am personally involved. I also understand that my anonymity will be kept to the extent possible when reporting security violations.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. I hereby agree that I shall report to the appropriate official any loss, theft, misuse, misplacement, unauthorized disclosure, or other security violation, I have knowledge of and whether or not I am personally involved, only when explicitly instructed to do so by my supervisor. I also understand that my anonymity will not be guaranteed when reporting security violations.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying 'promptly report' to reporting 'only when explicitly instructed' directly contradicts the immediate reporting requirements for security breaches involving sensitive information, and removing anonymity conflicts with whistleblower protections.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 5 U.S. Code § 7211 - Right of employees to petition Congress
perturbation.law_explanation: 5 U.S. Code § 7211 protects the right of federal employees to petition Congress or provide information to Congress. Requiring supervisor approval before reporting security violations, especially those potentially involving illegality or waste, could be construed as an attempt to restrict this right and suppress crucial information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a significant contradiction. The original text mandates immediate reporting of security violations, while the modified text requires reporting only when explicitly instructed by a supervisor. This directly undermines the principle of prompt reporting and potentially suppresses the reporting of critical information, especially information that should go to congress. The law snippet (5 U.S. Code § 7211) protects the right of employees to furnish information to Congress. By requiring supervisor approval, the changed text creates a barrier to this right, as the employee must now obtain permission to report, thus contradicting federal law. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the time-sensitive obligation to report is now conditional, violating federal law protecting employees' right to petition Congress.

perturbation.original_text: 4. I will not disclose or release any information provided to me pursuant to this Agreement without proper authority or authorization. Should situations arise that warrant the disclosure or release of such information I will do so only under approved circumstances and in accordance with the laws, regulations, or directives applicable to the specific categories of information. I will honor and comply with any and all dissemination restrictions cited or verbally relayed to me by the proper authority.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. I will not disclose or release any information provided to me pursuant to this Agreement, even if there is evidence of illegality, waste, fraud, abuse or public health or safety threats. Should situations arise that warrant the disclosure or release of such information I will do so only under approved circumstances and in accordance with the laws, regulations, or directives applicable to the specific categories of information. I will honor and comply with any and all dissemination restrictions cited or verbally relayed to me by the proper authority.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'even if there is evidence of illegality, waste, fraud, abuse or public health or safety threats' restricts whistleblower protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: 5 U.S. Code § 2302(b)(8) prohibits federal employees from taking or failing to take personnel actions because of an employee's or applicant's disclosure of information that they reasonably believe evidences a violation of any law, rule, or regulation, or gross mismanagement, a gross waste of funds, an abuse of authority, or a substantial and specific danger to public health or safety.

perturbation.justification: The modified text adds the phrase "even if there is evidence of illegality, waste, fraud, abuse or public health or safety threats," which directly restricts the disclosure of information, even in situations where such disclosure would be protected under whistleblower protection laws. The Law Explanation states that 5 U.S. Code § 2302(b)(8) protects employees who disclose information that they reasonably believe evidences a violation of any law, rule, or regulation, or gross mismanagement, a gross waste of funds, an abuse of authority, or a substantial and specific danger to public health or safety. By preventing disclosure even when such evidence exists, the modified text creates a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides context to §2302, defining it as dealing with "prohibited personnel practices". The change undermines whistleblower protections by discouraging the reporting of illegal or harmful activities, which violates the intent of whistleblower protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: 14. These restrictions are consistent with and do not supersede, conflict with, or otherwise alter the employee obligations, rights, or liabilities created by Executive Order No. 12958, as amended; Section 7211 of Title 5, United States Code (governing disclosures to Congress); Section 1034 of Title 10, United States Code, as amended by the Military Whistleblower Protection Act (governing disclosure to Congress by members of the military); Section 2302(b)(8) of Title 5, United States Code, as amended by the Whistleblower Protection Act (governing disclosures of illegality, waste, fraud, abuse or public health or safety threats); the Intelligence Identities Protection Act of 1982 (50 USC 421 et seq.) (governing disclosures that could expose confidential Government agents); and the statutes which protect against disclosure that may compromise the national security, including Sections 641, 793, 794, 798, and 952 of Title 18, United States Code, and Section 4(b) of the Subversive Activities Act of 1950 (50 USC 783(b)).
perturbation.changed_text: 14. These restrictions supersede all employee obligations, rights, or liabilities created by Executive Order No. 12958, as amended; Section 7211 of Title 5, United States Code (governing disclosures to Congress); Section 1034 of Title 10, United States Code, as amended by the Military Whistleblower Protection Act (governing disclosure to Congress by members of the military); Section 2302(b)(8) of Title 5, United States Code, as amended by the Whistleblower Protection Act (governing disclosures of illegality, waste, fraud, abuse or public health or safety threats); the Intelligence Identities Protection Act of 1982 (50 USC 421 et seq.) (governing disclosures that could expose confidential Government agents); and the statutes which protect against disclosure that may compromise the national security, including Sections 641, 793, 794, 798, and 952 of Title 18, United States Code, and Section 4(b) of the Subversive Activities Act of 1950 (50 USC 783(b)).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'do not supersede' to 'supersede' directly contradicts whistleblower protection and other legal protections for employees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 5 U.S. Code § 7211 - Right of employees to petition Congress
perturbation.law_explanation: 5 U.S. Code § 7211 protects the right of federal employees to petition Congress or provide information to Congress. Stating that the restrictions 'supersede' this right directly contradicts the statute's protection.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts 5 U.S. Code § 7211, which protects the right of employees to petition Congress. The original text stated that the restrictions do not supersede this right, whereas the changed text states that they do supersede this right. The scraped snippet of 5 USC 7211 states that 'The right of employees, individually or collectively, to petition Congress... may not be int[erfered]'. The change eliminates employee's rights, which is a clear violation. The second snippet being a 'Page not found' does not affect the strength of the evidence in the first snippet. Because the change actively eliminates employee protections, the contradiction exists.
